title: Induced Subgraphs and Pathwidth
speaker: Maria Chudnovsky
abstract:
Tree decompositions, and in particular path decompositions, are a powerful tool in both structural graph theory and graph algorithms. Many hard problems become tractable if the input graph is known to have a tree decomposition of bounded “width”. Exhibiting a particular kind of a tree decomposition is also a useful way to describe the structure of a graph.
Families of bounded treewith and pathwidth have been completely characterized in terms of forbidden subgraphs (and minors) in the 1990s.  Studying this question in connection with graph containment relations of more local flavor (such as induced subgraph or induced minors) is a relatively new research direction. In this talk we will present a recent result that provides a complete list of induced subgraph obstructions to bounded pathwidth.
This is joint work with Sepehr Hajebi and Sophie Spirkl, building on earlier results of the series "Induced subgraphs and tree decompositions", that is currently comprised of 18 papers.

proof_stages.label: Tree and path decompositions as width parameters
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines tree decompositions, path decompositions, treewidth, and pathwidth, and explains their intuition through bag size, balanced separators, overlapping local pieces, and algorithmic dynamic programming.

proof_stages.label: Exclusion questions and classical minor/subgraph theorems
proof_stages.summary: The central question is framed as asking which forbidden configurations force bounded width, with the Robertson--Seymour grid/wall theorem and its subgraph subdivision version recalled after defining minors and induced minors.

proof_stages.label: Induced-treewidth obstructions and brick reduction
proof_stages.summary: For induced subgraphs one must forbid complete graphs, complete bipartite graphs, subdivided walls, and line graphs of subdivided walls, but counterexamples show these basic obstructions are insufficient, leading to results that reduce the remaining large-treewidth case to clique-like multi-path brick subdivisions.

proof_stages.label: Additional nonbasic large-treewidth families
proof_stages.summary: The brick reduction motivates further necessary obstruction families, including orderly multi-path constructions with external vertices hitting the paths and the death-star construction, which become the Y-type obstructions.

proof_stages.label: Pathwidth reduced to treewidth after excluding X-obstructions
proof_stages.summary: The pathwidth analogue replaces walls by binary trees for minors/subgraphs, and Higginbotham's theorem is used to say that after excluding the induced pathwidth-basic X-obstructions, bounded treewidth is enough to bound pathwidth.

proof_stages.label: Main induced-subgraph characterization for bounded pathwidth
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: for every t, forbidding the X_t obstructions and the generalized Y_t obstruction families as induced subgraphs forces bounded pathwidth.

proof_stages.label: Large induced complete bipartite minor case
proof_stages.summary: The proof first analyzes a large induced K_{r,r} minor by studying minimal connected branch pieces hitting many opposite branch sets, whose few possible shapes force one of the forbidden X/Y obstructions.

proof_stages.label: Brick, banana, and contraction argument
proof_stages.summary: In the absence of such induced complete bipartite minors, large treewidth gives a brick subdivision; Ramsey-type choices of induced paths, unbreakable bananas, and induced-minor contractions produce smaller bricks and finally a forbidden tree induced minor, contradicting the strengthened exclusions and hence bounding treewidth and pathwidth.

examples.name: Rooted-tree decomposition of a tree
examples.note: A tree is decomposed by using the same underlying tree and putting each vertex together with its parent toward a chosen root, making the definition of bags and connectedness concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker constructs the decomposition for a rooted tree, choosing root 3, tracing paths to the root, and listing the resulting bags.

passes.reason: a tree decomposed by essentially itself is the minimal treewidth base case, with bags of size at most an edge and hence optimal for a nontrivial tree.

examples.name: Trivial one-bag decomposition
examples.note: The audience-observed decomposition with all vertices in one bag motivates why the width must minimize bag size rather than merely ask for a representation.
